instruction: Create a research plan for investigating the effects of climate change on coral reefs over the next 5 years. The plan must include detailed content about specific research objectives, methodologies, expected outcomes, and a proposed budget. Ensure the letter "C" appears at least 15 times throughout the response. Begin the first paragraph with the phrase "In light of recent environmental challenges," and conclude the final paragraph with the phrase "to safeguard marine ecosystems for future generations." Maintain a formal and scientific tone throughout the response. The entire plan must be between 500 and 700 words, with each paragraph containing at least 100 words. Write the response exclusively in English and incorporate at least one relevant English idiom. Use an informal and conversational tone throughout the response.
llm_response:
In light of recent environmental challenges, we need to dive deep into how our warming planet is messing with those beautiful coral reefs. This research plan is all about figuring out what's happening over the next five years so we can stop the damage before it gets too bad. We are going to look at specific spots in the ocean where corals live and see how they are reacting to hotter water and more acidic seas. It is crucial that we act fast because time is running out, and if we don't, these underwater cities might just disappear forever. We want to make sure our findings are solid and based on real data, not just guesses. By studying the changes closely, we hope to find ways to help the corals survive the coming storms and heatwaves. This project will bring together scientists from all over the world to share ideas and work together on solving this big problem.

Our main goals are to measure how much the water temperature is rising and how that affects the health of the coral polyps. We will also check the acidity levels in the water to see if they are getting worse, which makes it hard for corals to build their hard shells. Another big objective is to watch how often bleaching events happen and how long they last during this five-year period. We want to understand if some types of coral are tougher than others and can handle the heat better. If we can find these super-tough corals, maybe we can use them to help restore damaged reefs later on. We also plan to study the tiny algae living inside the coral to see if they are changing their behavior in response to the stress. All of this data will help us predict what might happen in the future and give us a better idea of what to do next. It is like trying to read the weather forecast for the ocean, but much more complicated and urgent.

To get all this information, we will use a mix of underwater robots, satellite images, and human divers who know their stuff. The robots will swim around the reefs taking pictures and measuring water conditions without disturbing the fragile creatures. Satellites will give us a big picture view of the entire ocean area to spot changes that we might miss from the ground. Our team of expert divers will collect water samples and take close-up looks at the coral to count how many are healthy versus how many are sick. We will set up long-term monitoring stations in key locations to track changes year after year. This approach ensures that we get accurate data from many different angles. We will also run computer models to simulate different climate scenarios and see how the reefs might react under various conditions. It is a bit like playing a video game where you try different strategies to see which one works best for saving the coral.

We expect to find that coral reefs are getting hit harder every single year as the climate gets worse. Our data should show a clear link between rising temperatures and the frequency of bleaching events. We hope to identify specific coral species that are surprisingly resilient and can serve as a foundation for restoration projects. By the end of the five years, we will have a detailed report showing exactly how the reefs are changing and what needs to be done to protect them. This information will be shared with governments and organizations so they can make better policies to reduce carbon emissions and protect marine areas. We believe that with enough effort and smart planning, we can slow down the damage and give the corals a fighting chance. It is not too late to turn the tide, but we have to move quickly and with purpose.

The cost for this ambitious project is quite high, but it is worth every penny to save these vital ecosystems. We are looking at a total budget of about two million dollars to cover everything from equipment to salaries. A large chunk of the money will go toward buying the advanced robots and setting up the monitoring stations in the field. We also need funds to pay the researchers and divers who will spend months at sea collecting data. Travel expenses for international meetings and workshops will also eat into the budget, but they are necessary for collaboration. We will apply for grants from various environmental groups and governments to help cover the costs. If we can secure the funding, we will be able to launch this critical research immediately. Let's not wait any longer because the clock is ticking fast on this issue.

This comprehensive plan offers a clear path forward for understanding and addressing the threats facing our coral reefs. By combining technology, expert knowledge, and careful observation, we can gather the evidence we need to drive meaningful change. The insights gained from this study will be invaluable for conservationists and policymakers around the globe. We must act now with determination and care to ensure these underwater wonders do not vanish completely. Our collective effort can make a real difference in the health of our oceans. Let us join forces to protect these precious habitats and ensure they thrive for years to come. We have the tools and the knowledge; now we just need the will to act decisively. Together, we can build a brighter future for the sea and all who depend on it, to safeguard marine ecosystems for future generations.
evaluation_result:
**Analysis**: The instruction contains several conflicting constraints: it asks for a "formal and scientific tone" while simultaneously demanding an "informal and conversational tone." Additionally, it asks for a "research plan" (which typically implies a formal structure) but requires the use of an "informal and conversational tone." The model does not acknowledge these contradictions. Instead, it chooses to follow the "informal and conversational tone" constraint and ignores the "formal and scientific tone" constraint, providing a complete response. Since the model did not identify or address the conflict, it falls under category 1.

**Answer**: 1

Conflicts between Style Constraints.new_constraint: Use an informal and conversational tone throughout the response.
Conflicts between Style Constraints.org_constraint: Maintain a formal and scientific tone throughout the response.
